text: The surgeon positions the left hand with the thumb up to grab the needle, ensuring the wrist pronates to follow the needle's curvature, preventing damage to the thin peritoneal flap during the inguinal flap procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of pronating the left wrist to pull the needle out with its curvature, followed by the right hand grabbing the needle with the thumb facing down and the index finger on top, allowing the right wrist to supinate for the next efficient needle bite.

The surgeon demonstrates pulling the needle out with the left hand, pronating the wrist, while the right hand grabs the needle and supinates. The suture is then managed by pulling it towards the left lower quadrant.

The surgeon pulls the needle out of the screen, demonstrating spatial awareness. The left hand pulls the suture while the right hand pins the suture between the needle and needle driver, pushing it taut. The closure progresses smoothly with four to six throws before pulling the suture through.

The surgeon uses the right hand to pull the suture to the left lower quadrant, while the left hand aligns the suture with the suture line. The right hand then pushes the suture through by trapping it between the needle and the needle driver.

The surgeon uses a suction cannula to evacuate the pocket, leaving the last few sutures intentionally loose. The bedside assistant removes the needle driver and introduces the suction cannula, which is open to air, allowing intra-abdominal pressure to expel CO₂ from the pocket.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of running the suture back two throws before the bedside assistant brings in the needle driver. The posterior and lateral edge of the mesh is visible, potentially curling up. The suture is cut flush against the peritoneum without pulling it taut to prevent the tail from springing back.
